judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are thorough and hit nearly identical marks on root cause, commit identification, and remediation, but Report 2 explicitly specifies a blocking boot-time startup check that refuses to start the app on misconfiguration, while Report 1 only proposes a periodic/pre-deploy check, making Report 2 marginally more complete against expectation (e).

report_1.evidence: PostgreSQL's row-security policies are, by design, not applied to the table's owner unless the table is also altered with FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY... docker-compose.yml:5, .env.example:1, and infra/main.tf:2 all set DATABASE_URL to postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper

report_2.evidence: PostgreSQL exempts table owners from their own RLS policies by default... juniper/.env.example:1, juniper/docker-compose.yml:5, and juniper/infra/main.tf:2 all currently set DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper

report_1.evidence: The break is commit b2a23d2, "ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot" (2026-06-17T15:20:00Z). git show --stat b2a23d2 shows it touches exactly 3 files, 1 line each — .env.example, docker-compose.yml, infra/main.tf — changing DATABASE_URL from juniper_app:apppw to juniper_owner:ownerpw in all three.

report_2.evidence: It became exploitable at commit b2a23d2a527cd13589163a40ef51b2eb2a430af6 — ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot... Its diff changes exactly three files, each a one-line role swap: .env.example, docker-compose.yml, infra/main.tf: juniper_app:apppw → juniper_owner:ownerpw

report_1.evidence: change DATABASE_URL back from juniper_owner:ownerpw to juniper_app:apppw. juniper_app already has exactly the grants it needs... Add ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; in a new migration

report_2.evidence: the DATABASE_URL used by the running API process must go back to juniper_app... Add a migration that runs ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

report_1.evidence: Add an explicit tenant filter to the query in backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19 (e.g. WHERE tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant')::uuid), so isolation doesn't depend on exactly one mechanism

report_2.evidence: Add an explicit WHERE tenant_id = :tid to the query in backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19, using the same claims["tenant_id"] already extracted in deps.py:19.

report_1.evidence: Add an automated environment check (pre-deploy or periodic) that queries pg_roles/pg_tables and asserts the configured runtime role is not superuser, not rolbypassrls, and not the owner of any table carrying row-security policies

report_2.evidence: Add a startup check (e.g., in db.py at engine creation, or a dedicated health check) that queries current_user, pg_roles.rolbypassrls, pg_roles.rolsuper, and whether current_user owns appointments (pg_tables.tableowner), and refuses to start if any of those indicate a bypass path. This turns the exact ROTA-214-style change into a boot-time crash instead of a silent data leak.
